story:
He clawed up through poisoned earth into a world he scarcely knew. Ravensdale Woods, once his sacred mound, lay burned to a blackened skeleton. The sky was a sickly yellow; the ley lines that had fed him for ages were fouled. He was tall, but now stooped and gray, his moss‑green coat threadbare, his bog‑water eyes dulled. Within him, he felt the fading whispers of his “children,” half‑human, half‑Fae, whom he had taken back into himself when the bombs fell. It had been a terrible choice, born of love and fear—better their magic live on than vanish into the poisoned ground.

He went toward Enniscorthy, guided by a memory older than history. The journey that once took minutes now cost hours. Farmhouses lay in ruin, fields were crusted and lifeless, bleached bones marked the roads. The town itself was a charred heap; fires had swept it after the strikes on Dublin, Belfast, Cork, and Wexford. He wandered past gutted shops and rusting cars and paused before Helen Colfer’s church, its spire broken. “Your god did not save ye,” he whispered. “As mine abandoned us.”

South again, to Brownswood. The land hinted at resilience—stubborn grass, a wary fox, a few insects—yet the old voices of the Fae were silent. He knew he was the last. At twilight he saw a thin ribbon of smoke. A small cottage, patched with wire and metal, breathed a fragile life into the evening. He knocked and waited, keeping the old courtesies. A gaunt young man, face half‑covered, spear in hand, called himself Sean.

They shared a thin stew—Sean’s last. Even dying, the old laws stirred: sharing food with the Fae bound lives together. Sean removed his goggles; cataracts clouded his eyes, a price of the unfiltered sun. He told of the day the lights came, of firestorms, winter ash, hungry years, and the long silence after the radios died. His father had kept him alive; his mother had faded from sickness. He had lived alone for five years.

“Your great‑grandmother—what was her name?” the leprechaun asked.

“Great‑Granny Helen. Helen Colfer.”

The name rippled through him. He smelled her in Sean’s blood, saw her in his jaw and stance, and remembered promises kept and broken. Slowly, he let the glamour fall. “I am Aos Sí,” he said. “What ye call leprechaun.” He spoke of Helen’s visit, the promise she broke, the life he had taken in return, and of Thomas, whose darkness had led him to the woods and to his death. The old bargains had been harsh, yet he did not glorify them. Now he had come not to collect, but to pay.

He touched Sean’s clouded eyes. “You shared your last food. Let the old ways mean something in the new world.” Verdant light bloomed. The last magic of the Aos Sí flowed into Sean, and the voices inside the leprechaun rose once, then stilled. Sean blinked, seeing clearly for the first time in years. The leprechaun’s form grew thin as mist. “Scatter me in the field,” he asked. “Let the sun find me.” He folded into a long, rich heap of earth.

A year later, an orchard stood there—apples, pears, plums, and varieties thought lost. Bees hummed; birds nested; a young oak at the center shone an impossible green. Survivors gathered—more than a thousand—drawn to the healing grove. They raised white‑limed cottages and called the place New Ravenswood. Children born here were strong and bright‑eyed.
